title: DAVID — Charlemagne: The Emperor Who Named the Months
logline: Biography of Charlemagne with the attested Frankish month-names Einhard records he assigned; Carolingian Latin context; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Einhard, Vita Karoli Magni (c. 829) — Charlemagne's native Frankish speech, his Latin learning, and the Frankish month and wind names he assigned
sources.notes: Eyewitness courtier biography; ch. 25 (learning) and ch. 29 (Frankish month names) are the direct attestations

sources.citation: Notker the Stammerer, Gesta Karoli Magni (c. 887) — anecdotal Carolingian court tradition
sources.notes: Later monastic compilation; legendary in tone, used only for court-culture context

sources.citation: Royal Frankish Annals (Annales regni Francorum) — reign chronology and campaigns
sources.notes: Official Carolingian annalistic record in Latin

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Charlemagne — king of the Franks, first emperor in the West since Rome, and a ruler who spoke one language and rebuilt another.
Born around seven forty-eight, he united most of western Europe by the sword and the pen, and on Christmas Day in the year eight hundred was crowned emperor in Rome.

His mother tongue was Frankish, a Germanic speech; the language of his law, his church, and his scholars was Latin — which he drove a whole renaissance to restore. Einhard, who knew him, wrote it all down.
Wintarmanoth, Hornung, Lentzinmanoth — winter-month, February, spring-month — the Frankish names Einhard says Charlemagne himself gave the calendar, in his own Germanic tongue.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED · Frankish · Einhard, Vita Karoli ch. 29

beats.speech_text: He died at Aachen in eight fourteen. What survives is not a recording, but a courtier's testimony — the emperor who could not quite learn to write, yet made the West write again.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Attested Frankish month-names recorded by Einhard — Germanic, not Latin
METHOD: Eyewitness biography (Einhard) + Carolingian annals and court tradition
